content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Fantastic film. Would not rewatch.

**Score:** 122
**Body:** Looking for movies that are flat out fantastic stories, or works of art… Something that was really impactful, but for whatever reason you could never rewatch. I’m thinking along the lines of City of God, Requiem for a Dream, Irreversible, etc. 

Whatcha got?

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Dancer in the Dark
2. Grave of the fireflies. I’m not much of an anime fan but this one was incredible and hit all the right spots. But I don’t think I’d ever want to see it again.
3. Nocturnal Animals is a fantastic film I will never watch again.

Edit: and The Road, I'm good with never seeing that again but damn it was good.
4. Manchester by the Sea
5. We need to talk about Kevin. Tilda swinton of course is astounding but it is so dense and intense, once is good
6. Schindler's List.
7. Dear Zachary. It broke me.
8. I'll give something I doubt anyone else will.  Steel magnolias 

I've lost a alot of family to cancer / sickness and Sally Field gives probably the most realistic portrayal of a grieving mother who finally cracks at the end

The movie made me laugh , care for the characters all while you can see the pain coming a mile away kinda makes it hurt all the worse. 


Amazing movie imo and hits hard if you relate. Plus, who doesn't love Dolly Parton
9. 12 Years a Slave, Schindler’s List, Blue is the Warmest Color, Boyz in the Hood, Higher Learning
10. The Whale

## 2. Guillermo del Toro - Frankenstein

**Score:** 37
**Body:** I delayed watching this movie because of some middling reviews. I've decided del Toro is perhaps the greatest filmmaker in my lifetime. This movie is gorgeous, as are all of his films, as well as poignant and thought-provoking. I can't stop thinking about it. I was a bit of a Mia Goth eye-roller, but am now a fan. I'm skeptical of modern literary interpretations but find this one gorgeous. I honestly can't say enough about this film. "The Shape of Water" is probably the most beautiful film I've ever seen, but this is cĺose and much more ambitious.

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The art direction was incredible, Goths clothes in particular.
  - Her whole wardrobe carried so much of the vibe it felt like every outfit told its own little story and tied the whole mood together so cleanly
  - I told my husband I want all of her costumes.
    - It’s an academy award for costumes right there.
2. Hmmmm, yeah . . .  I loved looking at it, visually it was gorgeous, but I felt absolutely nothing as the story was going on.  I thought the doctor's immediate disdain for his creation was really horrible, like chaining him in the basement the second the poor thing wakes up, not giving him clothes or anything.  And then not teaching him anything and then being pissed that he doesn't know anything is just so awful.  I didn't feel anything for the doctor, and I didn't feel anything for Elizabeth, who was just too perfect.  No flaws or bad decisions at all.  Looks perfect, acts perfect, has perfect and immediate empathy for the creature, etc.  

And don't get me started on the whole "picking up the skull and holding it like Hamlet" heavyhanded moment.  I just rolled my eyes again thinking about it.
  - It made me feel contempt for the terrible script. The “characters” were crudely drawn caricatures at best, whose every action had nothing to do with eternal consistency and everything to do with driving the ludicrous cliche   Ridden plot forward. But ya other than that it made me feel nothing.
3. Boring film, awful digital photography, lacking soul, exaggerated performances, del Toro self-plagiarizing the worst of his work. I do not believe the excessive praise for a product that looks like a video game cinematic.
  - yeah.... i love del toro and had high hopes.  but frankenstein fucking sucked.  boring, predictable, hamfisted.  if that's the story, no amount of cool costumes or set designs matter to me at all.
4. Pros: Good costumes, great cast, atmospheric colour palette. That's about all the good things I can say about the film. 



Cons: Flat characters - dead-behind-the-eyes Elizabeth, who falls in love with everyone who comes across her path. 


Victor, whose scientific interest fades away seconds after he'd achieved his lifetime goal. What was the purpose of that tantrum in the castle? Has he gone mad? No. Later we see Victor and he is fine.


The Creature - immortal, enlightened, and very moral, yet kills half of the crew of the Horisont instead of talking to them. It wouldn't be a problem if Ctearure's rage fit would last till it kills Victor, but it stops abruptly just because. Hunters that 'killed' the Creature for the second time and just left it lying by their front gate: we shot this thing before and it just came back and killed Gramps. We shoot it again and just let it be. What can go wrong?


Painful CGI. Good thing they didn't run this film in the theatres. 


The film has no business being as long as it is. It can shake off 30 minutes of screentime without affecting quality 



Overall, Poor Things did a better job at being Frankenstein than Frankenstein.
5. It's classic literature made for the Marvel generation

## 3. Good documentary please

**Score:** 33
**Body:** Ones I’ve liked are Impostor, The Staircase, the Jinx. So something like those would be great. Not interested in bios, animals, bigfeet, ufo type stuff.

Thanks in advance.

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Making a Murderer, season 1. Reminded me of The Staircase in the sense that it follows someone’s case over many years

“Capturing the Friedmans”

“There’s something wrong with Aunt Diane”

“Trial by Media”
  - I just watched something wrong with aunt Diane this morning and I got to say. That family was in extreme denial about Diane. I’ve personally had my fair share of addiction problems and rest assured my loved ones didn’t know for the longest. So it’s not far fetched that they didn’t know she drank and smoked pot. I can also attest to tooth pain going from 1 to 100 in an instant and to the point where couldn’t see. But tbh. She was going the wrong way on the freeway with extreme amount of alcohol in her system so idk maybe she snapped.
2. Grizzly Man
3. Tickled 🪶
4. I highly recommend the 'Paradise Lost' trilogy, about the child murders in Arkansas. 'West of Memphis' is a much better documentary as far as the facts go, but the experience of watching the 'Paradise Lost' films is invaluable in that your perception of the case will change multiple times. You will become absolutely certain of who the killer is and later find out what really happened and how people suffered because others had the same false certainty. Because of its faults, rather than in spite of them, I think the trilogy should be required viewing for anyone interested in true crime.
5. Murder On A Sunday Morning (2001)
6. Just Melvin,Just Evil. 
Dear Zachary. 
The murdaugh murders.
  - Dear Zachary can literally put you to bed with grief
7. Don't fuck with cats
8. Man On Wire

## 4. a christmas movie you love

**Score:** 17
**Body:** i have some time to kill and absolutely want to watch a christmas movie. Can you guys suggest me one that you personally really love. Not only it’s because a classic but also because it means something to you. I am also open to suggestions of movies where it’s not the typical christmas movie, but it is in your heart. *cough* means girls
thanks a lot and appreciate you all 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Die Hard
2. Always and forever: **It's a Wonderful Life**

Watch it every Christmas eve. 🎅🎄🤶
3. A Christmas Carol but only the one with George C Scott.
  - Michael Caine and the Muppets!
4. Scrooged
5. *The Holiday* (2006)

*While You Were Sleeping* (1995)
6. It's cheesy and formulaic but I really like The Family Stone. It's a great ensemble cast.
7. Have you seen Klaus? its cozy, heartfelt and way more meaningful. Great pick if you want 👌
8. Nobody's Fool (1994)


Phenomenal cast, heartfelt story, and it covers Thanksgiving through New Year's.
9. White Christmas.  My father was in the army during WWII so the movie was something the whole family could relate to.

## 5. Horror/comedy movie where the human is worse than the demon/ghost

**Score:** 17
**Body:** So I saw a kinda bad horror movie the other day where the demon haunting the girl was really bad, yk basic demon stuff, and then I realized how much more interesting it would be if the girl was like ten times worse, like the demon says it'll kill anyone the girl talks to, so she uses that and starts talking with everyone on the neighborhood up to the point the demon asks her to stop.  
  
I know it's a silly idea but I wasn't able to find anything like that on google, it could be horror, comedy, long, short, whatever, it could even be a TV series! I just really want to see if someone had this idea and ended up making something interesting with that!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. A while back, someone posted an idea for a movie in which aliens abduct a man who turns out fo be a maniacal serial killer hunting and taunting them. Fun concept.
2. Spolier for the 2014 film >!Housebound. Turns out the ghost is just a missing person living in the walls, who wants to be left alone. The real monster is the corrupt psychologist.!<
3. I’d say Death Note kinda fits here if you’ve never seen it, it’s the only anime i can recommend to any person who isn’t interested in anime
4. Humans plural instead of human singular but the movie Abigail fits this.
5. Nightbreed
6. Tucker & Dale vs Evil.

What we do in the shadows.
7.  Horns. When he’s first given the power, he uses it in bad ways at first
8. Aliens

At least you don't see them fucking each over for a goddamn percentage.
9. Drag me to hell came to mind
10. Basically anything by Stephen King. He always includes a human antagonist that at some point makes you think "well the creepy monster from another dimension is just trying to survive the best it knows how, but this human asshole is just a straight-up sadist".

---

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Which of these actresses has the best filmography?

**Score:** 1075

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Stone is building up her auteur portfolio now, and kiling at it. I am excited for her. She's one I knew would be a star from her first movie, and it's cool to see the choices she makes with her own taste to guide her. She's weird, and it's awesome.

ScarJo is a mainstay ultra A list forever, from her early casting in The Man Who Wasn't There, and especially now, given her box office numbers. She's not gone down the high drama path much, but she has nothing to prove from an artistic standpoint. She can act. She is Black Widow and Under the Skin and Marriage Story. Just an all around tank of an actor.

JLaw is consistent, but has been less interesting. I assume she's considering some kind of career pivot; I would be if I were her, or her manager. She's smart but sometimes cruel, limited in range, and importantly, she does not seem to have a strong personal taste. Sometimes, in her interviews, I wonder if she's mildly on the spectrum. But she is very, VERY funny.  If Woody Allen was unproblematic and in his heyday today, she'd be cast in every one of his films and have ten Oscars by now.

Hathaway is also consistent, obviously less funny than Jlaw, more dramatic and period drama, and should have probably leaned into her theater kid roots more to leverage her niche. She has headlined significant IPs, and she is a real talent with rare screen presence. Still, she's not at the level Cate & Kate were at her age, the two great actresses of our time imho, and by all other metrics she seems like she *should* have been up there. But she isn't. 

Emily Blunt is always a surprise; she is always more likeable, more capable, and more talented than you remember—oddly, kind of like Tom Cruise, who nowadays I always think of in the context of scientology, and am pleasantly confused and surprised by how good he is in an actual movie. Blunt is the same. She needs to headline more original IP, I think; her image is in desperate need of an iconic character. If Edge of Tomorrow had taken off the way it deserved, that would have been it. But if didn't, and Mary Poppin wasn't going to do it for her either, so all we have left is The Devil Wears Prada, which...is a very old film at this point.

This didn't answer your question at all.

Edit: forgot Robbie. Here you go:

Margot is fascinating because she’s obviously a dish with real, real talent (rare!) but she is apparently also a sound businessperson who wants to be deeply involved in the act of financing and creating films.

This only became really obvious with Barbie, and now she has the money and funds to do whatever she wants forever. It’s telling her next step was to partner with an amazing up and coming—but somewhat uneven and controversial—auteur, Emerald Fennell, on an offbeat, modern take of a problematic IP. This is not just moviemaking, this is a move to pave new stylizations of cinema, like Miramax and A24.

Like Johansson or di Caprio, Robbie is an all-around solid actor who can handle most anything, but that’s the least interesting thing about her right now. It’s clear her sights are on leading a genre expanding production company. I don’t know many others who are so well set up now to succeed. I think she can do it.
  - Enjoyed reading this. Have a swing at Margot cos I’m interested in your opinion 
  - You didn’t mention Margot Robbie
2. Emma’s is very versatile. 

She has done some great comedies with Easy A, Superbad and Zombieland, some crowd-pleasers like Cruella and The Amazing Spider-Man duology, she’s done her art house stuff with Yorgos, even gone the Oscar-bait route with The Help and Battle Of The Sexes. She kind of covers all the bases. 

That being said though, Margot probably has my favourite films of any of these, OUATIH and Babylon being frontrunners.
  - Emma also was in the house bunny
3. Emma Stone has nice diverse portfolio with very challenging roles with characters that are very complex (lately).  
On a side note, they are gorgeous in these pictures

edit: damn i didnt expect my low quality post to get so many likes!
  - She's batting a 1000 on getting the choicest, most demanding roles, and knocking them out of the park. It's really pretty impressive.
    - Poor things could have been a creepy disaster, but her performance made it good weird instead of weird weird. 

Shes fantastic.
  - Absolutely agree. They’re all great but Emma Stone is amazing.
4. It's Emma with all of the films already mentioned alone, and that's not even including her role in Birdman. All the rest are good, Emma stands out.

## 2. There is no Cher in Ba Sing Se.

**Score:** 850

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. She was an actress before she was even a singer lol
  - Dan "let me rub and lick clean those 15yo girl feet" Schneider is the reason we ain't talking about her acting career before singing.
    - Gotta tell ya, that is a very unfortunate nickname for a person
2. Frank Sinatra 

Will Smith 

Mark Wahlberg

Ice Cube

Justin Timberlake
  - Alot of rappers have become pretty decent actors

. Cube, 2pac, common, ll cool j, ice t, Ludacris, method man, rza, mos def and even 50 cent.
    - Although he hasn't been in a little of things Eminem was great in 8mile and is funny in a lot of his short cameos.
3. Was she not an actress before a singer
4. Jamie Fox would count too right? Stand up comedian, Musician, actor
5. Iggy Pop is a pretty decent actor.
6. Whitney Houston?

Cher? - She won an Oscar

## 3. Movie endings that left you with an empty feeling.

**Score:** 844
**Body:** The image is from the movie "Eternal Sunshine Of The Spotless Mind".

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Mist. I genuinely needed a moment of silence after that one.
  - i remember thinking about how much of a depressing ending it was but could understand why it needed to happen. then the fucking army showed up and the ending became ten times more depressing. great movie tho lol
2. Manchester by the sea....  
what my boy Lee did to get this?? 😭💔
3. All Quiet on the Western Front (1973)
4. Gone, Baby, Gone. 

Emptiness. Absolute emptiness.
5. The road 😐
6. SE7EN - Gut Punch
7. Eden Lake
8. Never Let Me Go

Oldboy

Come and See
9. Requiem for a Dream.

## 4. Give an example of an antagonist who is not a villain.

**Score:** 569
**Body:** Chuck McGil. He is one of the main antagonists of the show Better Call Saul. But is he a villain? Not at all. Chuck is an ordinary person with his own strengths and weaknesses, just like any of us.

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. [deleted]
  - If you all remember Saul (Jimmy) thought it was Howard that was preventing him from joining the firm when he first started. In fact it was Chuck that wouldn’t allow it and Howard actually had pushed to give Jimmy a chance. Howard took the brunt because Chuck wouldn’t own up to it at first.
2. Pierce Brosnan’s character in Mrs Doubtfire.
  - I love the scene at the bar where the movie goes out of its way to point out that he is not a POS and actually loves the kids.
    - And the cherry on top is Robin Williams and Sally Field not getting back together as a couple, but still reconciling and trusting in the other to co-parent safely and respectfully with the other
3. Tom Hanks in *Catch Me If You Can*
4. Deputy Sam Gerard by Tommy Lee Jones in the Fugitive (1993)
  - I don’t care
5. Ice Man, from Top Gun.

When you look back on it, you realize he was right all along and was guiding Maverick toward being a better pilot.
6. The BF from the Bee Movie

## 5. Happy 60th Birthday to Mads Mikkelsen

**Score:** 195

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. happy 60th to Mads Mikkelsen! He’s one of those actors who can completely own a scene without saying much, and that’s such a rare talent.
2. One of the modern GOATs.
3. He’s gotta be related to Tilda Swinton!!!
4. I remember cringing at the thought of another Hannibal Lector. I just watched Hannibal for the first time recently and I was so wrong. He was amazing. I wish they would make another Hannibal film that follows the book with Mads and Eva Green as Clarice.
5. One of the greats!

Happy Birthday!
6. Is this sub literally just wishing actors happy birthday?
7. Great in Hannibal
8. He's fucking great, The Hunt is a fabulous movie. Him not being Doctor Doom is bizarre.
9. Great dancer.
10. *swoons*

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Apocalypse now

**Score:** 18
**Body:** Just watched the Redux version for the first time and needless to be said, it is an astounding achievement of moving pictures imo. What I want to discuss about is the intention of Colonel Kurtz (or what drove him to insanity). He is someone who is at the top of his game in whatever he had done until then. Always in a class of his own and achieved pretty much all in his career. And the only achievement left for him to “achieve” is that near-perfect strength that comes from letting go of morality..like ditching the stuff that holds you back so you can act with absolute commitment and zero hesitation. And ofcos, when one acts on it, he IS insane. This is my interpretation of his insanity. What do you guys think?



### Top 3 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. His mind was tortured by the hypocrisy of the military mind and the horrors and contradictions of war itself. There's an indication of this in the scene where he says that pilots weren't allowed to write an obscene word on the sides of their planes (which would presumably then go on to bomb people, the actual obscenity.) His mind was fractured by moral crisis, having witnessed atrocities that he was at least partially responsible for. In Conrad's "Heart of Darkness," the novella which inspired the movie, Kurtz is driven made by the corrupting influence of absolute power and his own greed, which combined with the lack of constraints and isolation in the (African) jungle, contributes to his ruthlessness and mental decay. The Cambodian jungle in the film also represents a moral wilderness in which Kurtz's madness festered and worsened. However the larger meaning is that this is all metaphor for what America did to its own integrity and self-image during the Vietnam War years.
2. I agree with you, a lot of the movie is a criticism of the the way America was fighting the war. Trying to be civilized when war itself is inherently uncivilized. Kurt and Willard both witness the hypocrisy and endless lies of the US government which may have led to losing the war. Kurtz sees what it takes to win the war but also takes it too far to the point that he loses the ability to tell right from wrong (basically insanity). In the end, despite his respect for him, when Willard arrives he sees that Kurtz has crossed the line and Kurtz knows this too which is why he allows Willard to kill him. Part of what makes the film so brilliant to me is this complexity. I think it can be interpreted in many different ways. Some may see Kurtz as a monster but he could also be seen as an effective colonel in many ways.
  - That quote about “a man that turns himself into a monster removes his responsibility of being a man” always strikes me as the eternal problem for dedicated soldiers, and how society views them.
3. This isn't an answer to your question,  but I'm curious: is this your first time watch Apocalypse Now ever or your first time watching Redux specifically. 

Just wondering if you prefer Redux over the theatrical cut. For me Redux has a few good additions, but some stuff, especially the French plantation, kills the momentum.
  - Th French plantation is the only addition I hate from the Redux. The rest of it I love. I've watch this movie a dozen times in theaters, there was a little while in the late 80s/early 90s where it basically played every year at my local art house theater. Ive only seen Redux once I think, and it was recently in 4k on my 85 inch Samsung with a decent surround system (Apocalypse Now was one of the first movies to use a version of surround sound). It sounds sooooooooooooo good hella loud. And of course looks amazing, as I believe FFC himself oversaw the restoration (he has a little intro before the movie starts).
  - Yes. Decided to watch the Redux version first cos I read Coppola saying this was closer to his vision. Although I keep thinking how glorious it must have been to watch it during its theatrical run on a big ass screen. I was born 20 years late lol.
    - If you get a chance to watch an earlier cut, there is a lot more momentum in the story from taking out the helicopter scene after the USO concert and the French plantation.

## 2. Sentimental value cut scene

**Score:** 5
**Body:** I watched Sentimental Value at Cannes Festival. I’m 99% sure there was a literal hanging scene (1-2 seconds shot) but now when it’s released in theaters, there’s no scene. So I’m wondering if it was a Mandela effect. Have any of you seen it on the festival and can confirm or deny? Few of my friends who saw it in Cannes are claiming the same. Did any of you watch it and can confirm or deny?

### Top 1 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Hanging is one of my triggers, so my brother warns me of it in any films and he said in this one you "see the feet" or prep and know it's happening, but no full body shots. He saw it last week, general release. 

## 3. Fury Road is an anti-thesis of Hobbesian philosophy.

**Score:** 2
**Body:** I've just come across the philosopher Thomas Hobbes and it occurred to me that Fury Road and Furiosa flip the script on his ideas. The people of the Vuvalini live in an idyllic 'state of nature' - agrarian, communal. The chaos of the other world which on the surface resembles a Hobbesian nightmare is caused by the authoritarian leviathans - those who would govern.

### Top 3 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. What Hobbes would say is that you need one single power - the sovereign - strong enough to compel all parties within the state to cede violence to the sovereign. Hobbes would say that the world of Fury Road is precisely the nightmare - the war of every man against every man - that the Leviathan is there to prevent. Even if that sovereign power is morally bad, while it protects your physical life you are obliged to obey it because the alternative - being plunged back into the state of nature - is for him *always* worse than life under even the worst sovereign. All of this was based on his experience living through the English Civil War.

The fact that the Vuvalini can't protect themselves is, again, for Hobbes precisely the reason a guarantor of peace is required - again, this is the sovereign.
  - The society under sovereign rule in Fury Road is a dystopian nightmare. The Vuvalini are portrayed as highly capable, they are ultimately defeated by the environment. I think that Hobbes' view of the 'state of nature' as 'dog eat dog' is perhaps present in the original Mad Max but suspect that Miller has consciously pushed back on Hobbes in the sequels. It is the powerful that set man against man in the later films.
    - But the Vuvalini aren't under the control of the sovereign, they're independent and arguably suffer more than those under the sovereign. It's been a few years since I've seen the movie but does a single unnamed citizen of the citadel die in the movie? A few of the Vuvalini do. 

Sure, you can argue that that's the sovereign exacting violence, but it's against someone outside the sovereigns control trying to influence it with violence of their own. The sovereigns role is to maintain peace under their control, if Immortan were to let the Vuvalini do as they please he wouldn't be fulfilling his end of the social contract (to borrow language from Rousseau).
      - Are the people grovelling in the dirt for a drop of water citizens of the citadel? Are the warboys? The sovereigns are an oppressive force in Fury Road, not humanity's saviours. I think Miller is making a point there.
2. If I remember right Hobbes argued that people need strong ruler, Leviathan to save them from “war against all”. I think you can also view that dystopia as world after that “war against all” situation where despite of anarchic state someone oppurtunist ruler rise to power by exploiting people’s access to resources in Pavlovian way
3. I do feel like those movies pose an conter argument to most post apocaliptic fiction of 2010s(walking dead and the like) wich are almost always posing that individualism, each one for themselves, is the only way forward.
  - The walking dead has fundamentally the opposite thesis, the comics especially
  - They have a rich subtext and are quite thoughtful, not just there and back again as some say. The character theme in FR was 'engage to heal' - the characters move forward when they work together.

## 4. How do you personally go about watching LONG movies?

**Score:** 0
**Body:** For the final film in my Italian Cinema 1 Course, I will be watching The Best of Youth. Now, this is one I'm not feeling great about going in, and I'm not sure if I will be able to watch it not because of the contents of the film, but the marathon of a runtime: a whopping 366 minutes (over six hours). Now, the professor was out sick last week, so I'm planning on watching it next week (and also prepping for my final, a presentation to give in front of my fellow scholars) - though class will also be out, since that's the day us students will return home for Thanksgiving break.

Thankfully, I did check out the DVD from our college's library, and it is split onto two discs. The thing is, though, that still means I have to conquer two three-hour sessions. So, I have to ask: what's your strategy to watching long movies? And I'm not talking conventionally long (two and a half hours or more), but really long movies like the aforementioned Best of Youth, Fanny and Alexander (5 hours 14 minutes), and Satantango (7 hours 19 minutes), or multi-movie series like War and Peace (7 hours 11 minutes) and The Lord of the Rings (9 hours 18 minutes theatrical or 11 hours 23 minutes extended). And I say this with the longest movie I've ever sat through being Gone with the Wind, at a measly (by comparison) runtime of just under 4 hours. Any advice?

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Preparation: I rise promptly at sunrise, have a big bowl of Wheaties, do a bunch of chin-ups, put the phone on “silence all notifications,” put on a fresh pair of adult diapers, have a few empty 64 ounce gatorade thingies close at hand, make a fresh pot of coffee and then, only then do I sit down and press play. Lol.
2. I will split almost any movie into two (or even three) parts, if I want to see it, but don't have the time to watch it all in one sitting. It's not ideal, but I'd rather split it than miss it.

It can work really well. I get to spend a day thinking about the first half, and looking forward to the second.

Also I can think of a pretty common sense way of dividing the 9 hours 18 minutes of Lord of the Rings into three parts. I'm not going into details though, as it's a fairly open secret.
  - I wish new movies still had an intermission 😭
3. i mean, the best of youth is an easy one, really, as it was produced for television and is essentially that—a television miniseries. it's very easy to watch compared to, like andrei rublev or satantango.

some of these more 'artsy' films just require a conditioned attention span. you have to learn to slow your mind down, and learn to pay greater attention to details. because in slower, longer films, it's the details that are really everything. so in some respects, i find slower films, like the work of tarkovsky, much more immersive and interesting than an action movie, which are often 'empty calories' or they only have one idea spread across two hours. whereas slower films are often jam packed with visual ideas and dialogue that would have no place in a pop action film.

so that's just another way to think about your approach to watching longer films. they're more thoughtful, and they expect more thoughtfulness from you too. 

it also helps to do a bit of cultural and historical research before or during watching these films, as they are often not made for 'universal audiences' aka american audiences. they'll often discuss, in casual ways or brief references, incidents of deep significance to local audiences that would otherwise fly over your head. feeling compelled by my own ignorance to do this kind of research is just an added bonus to me.
4. I always assure I have time to watch it all in one sitting.  The makers intended it to be that length so I feel that you need to respect that intention.  I do not consider multi movie series as one movie
5. It's a great movie. I always looked at it as a TV mini series. So 6 x 1 hour sessions is easily done and as it's chronology is linear, spanning decades. Thus, you can take a break every hour and maybe look into the politics, events that occured in Italy at those times, which heavily influences the story and it's characters. 


That said, I watched it over 10 years ago and it one sitting, completely captivated and immersed in the story. Good luck, you got this!
6. watch untill fall asleep, restart on the next day from about two scenes before the last stuff I remember clearly, repeat as needed

or start it early in the morning, stop every two-to-three hours, whenever it starts feeling long and there's a scene that seems like a natural pause point, get up, stretch your legs, make breakfast, or lunch, or dinner, doomscroll for a bit maybe, go back to the movie when you're feeling fresh again. make it a full-day thing

or just get engrossed by the movie and watch it in one go. get surprised at the end that the day has turned into night and you didn't even notice. that's what i did with the human condion trilogy and it was kinda great
7. I know it’s difficult and you are at the mercy of a lot of outside factors but I really do best in a theater for a long film. Satantango was an ordeal but I would not have made it all the way through if I had the option to turn it off and move on at any point.
8. I can't speak to Best of Youth specifically, since I haven't watched it, but the fact that it's broken up into two discs, each one one half of the movie, is at least a start. My only advice is that there's no shame in splitting it up into separate viewing sessions, and to look up online where people recommend stopping for a bit. 

For example, the longest non-experimental film ever made is Resan, or The Journey, at a jaw-dropping 14 plus hours! However, the film is helpfully split up into chapters, each one being about 90 minutes each: makes it MUCH easier to watch, and even Watkins (RIP the GOAT) staged screenings of his movie like this over the course of several days. Fanny and Alexander was originally conceived as a miniseries, and it has been shown in it's entirety, in two parts, in 4 episodes, and in 5 episodes. From my understanding, the movie is split into several acts, so perhaps this is the best way to watch the movie without losing an entire half of the day. 

You mentioned Lord of the Rings, and I do want to share how I first saw it. It was last year, and me and my sister, having been oblivious to it for our entire lives, blocked out a weekend to see each movie, one every day. It was a fun time. War and Peace seems to be similarly spread out over several parts, and whenever I get around to it, that's how I will end up watching it. I'm not sure about Satantango; part of the experience seems to be about acknowledging he very real passage of time, and perhaps separate sessions will destroy that feeling. But most people don't usually have 7 hours to spare.
9. Three hours is no problem really - plenty of movies fall in that ballpark. Just think of this as two movies, both shorter than Gone With the Wind.

Generally I prefer to power through in one sitting if I can. My wife can happily watch a movie in five or six sittings, but I don’t share this skill. If there’s a natural break like a disc change or an intermission and I feel like I might drop off, I’ll grudgingly concede but I’d rather pause and make a coffee. The next day I can reflect on the pros and cons of putting a Jacques Rivette on at 11pm.

## 5. The Running Man and the Edgar Wright problem.

**Score:** 0
**Body:** Edgar Wright is his own worst enemy. Despite a reputation as a storied and beloved director, what has become abundantly clear in the past two decades is that his directing skills do not correlate with his writing skills. His two best films, Shaun of the Dead and Hot Fuzz, are both co-written by Simon Pegg. Much like Ricky Gervais' unfortunate demise into piss-poor "writing" with dross like Derek and After Life after he lost his writing partner Stephen Merchant, it's clear that Edgar Wright simply does not have what it takes to pen a good script on his own.

The Running Man has all the unfortunate pitfalls of latter-day Edgar Wright: piss-poor characterisation, awful humour, dialogue so choc-full of exposition that it might as well be a Wikipedia article for the film, a stymie sense of self-obsessed cool from Wright trying desperately to project his desire for people to congratulate him. 

But if you can cut through all that noise, and slice off the opening 20 minutes, which is some of the most interminable set-up, and the ending, which is the pinnacle of test-screening madness, then there is actually some fun to be had. Some of the action set pieces are thrilling, and when the film isn't tripping over itself in a desperate attempt at irony and satire, it manages to do okay.

The world-building is shoddy and inconsistent, and the satire is painfully outdated. The consistent "Americanos" (or whatever the fuck it was called) TV show that is doggedly pointed at the Kardashian style of reality TV is sheer low-hanging fruit, and there's really nothing clever about saying, in 2025, "gee, isn't some reality TV vapid?" It feels like a script that's been gathering dust since 2007, and he hasn't updated it at all. In Wright's feverish desire to translate the book 1:1 to a modern day film, he's forgotten that the elements of satire present in King's source novel (which I haven't read FYI) are now so commonplace in society today that satirising them feels like the equivalent of a comedian lampooning Britney Spears' shaved head in 2025. 

Oftentimes the frenetic pace is to the films detriment, as we move briskly past vignettes of characters and situations with so little time to dwell that nothing ever really sets in stone, and all this serves to do is muddy the world-building further. Is there a resistence? What is the average Joe like in this world? What is the public's relationship with TV and The Running Man? So little is said, despite it being so loudly said to us, that it's hard to make sense of, like being shouted at by a teacher in another language - we know they're shouting, we get they're angry, but why?

To refer back to the exposition, the most egregious example of this is in characterisation itself. Glen Powell's Ben is in a meeting with "the Network", and they tell him he's the angriest man in the world. Really? We don't really get any chance to see this outside of a few stupidly implausible moments, so Wright simply tells us this is the case. "Trust me guys, he's angry," I hear Wright whisper in my ear as he sits beside me in the cinema. I now just have to accept and integrate this on my own behalf into the film. It's clear at this point that Wright truly doesn't know how to write character, that the depth of the Cornetto trilogy is down to Simon Pegg, and that Wright's boring insistence on convincing us that he's so cool and obsessed with film does not translate to anything more than bang-average music-video aesthetics with the depth of a fucking lyric video. 

It's no wonder people love Baby Driver so much, because that film epitomises his shortcomings and finds a narrative justification for his stupid filmmaking techniques (though, for my money, it's truly one of the worst films ever made), but at least in The Running Man, King's source material has enough substance to it that Wright's woefully childish sensibilities can't completely choke it into disaster.

4. I largely agree with you (particuly about *Baby Driver*, which is an utterly pointless music video cosplaying as a film).

*Running Man* had a few dearly-wrought interesting moments but it's a complete mess, almost exclusively due to the script. The exposition manages to feel tedious and rushed all at the same time (the scene in Killian's office is utterly predictable, has no surprises, and still feels like Richards rolled over far too quickly). The characters are all over the place. Richards is supposed to be the Angriest Man in the World, but that trait vanishes as soon as he gets on the show (like endangering his own life to try to warn the goons about the basement filled with gas... WTF?) The film also requires us to simply look away and accept that Richards survived inescapable grisly death at least three times.

I recognize that third act is largely from the book, but, honestly, it didn't work in the book, either. In Wright's film nothing matters because The Network is all-powerful and Richards cannot escape them. None of this has *mattered*. And the end, where he relies on his ratings clout rather than his skill turns him from action hero to desperate slob. The Running Man spends a LOT of time as The Sitting Man. 

The entire epilogue is utterly ridiculous and defies belief. Glen Powell with his one-note performance and single constant expression of vague bewilderment does nothing to help.

Everything clever about this film was much more clever when Verhoeven did it first in *Robocop*.
  - Glad to see others agree. I feel like I'm insane because Baby Driver seems so universally beloved despite being so bad. The music video editing is so simple and so banal and I'm so surprised that that's all it takes to distract people from the glaring qualities.

But yes everything you said about Running Man rings true. It just felt like an AI telling me the synopsis of a film. There was zero agency or urgency to it. The good action set pieces work well in a vacuum but within the context of the film they're sitting in, they fall so flat.
